attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old man is brought in by ambulance to the emergency department with acute epigastric pain and drowsiness. He has a long standing history of gastro-oesophageal reflux and arthritis for which he takes ibuprofen and has a 20 pack-year smoking history while his alcohol intake consists of 3-4 standard drinks each night.

On examination he appears cool and clammy at the peripheries, he has severe epigastric tenderness with signs of peritonism and he is haemodynamically unstable with a blood pressure of 85/60 mmHg and a pulse rate of 130/min. He is diagnosed to have a perforated peptic ulcer which is managed with an urgent laparotomy.

Post-operatively he recovers well, however four days later he becomes confused and disoriented. On examination, his airway is patent but he is obviously dyspnoeic and tachypnoeic. His blood pressure is 115/65 mmHg, pulse rate 100/min, SpO2 91%, and temperature 38.4°C. There is dullness to percussion at the right base with reduced breath sounds.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following should be the first step in management?
options: A one litre bolus of intravenous saline
attributes.explanation:
Whilst it will be important to proceed with a number of investigations to try and determine the underlying cause of this patient's confusion, the most immediate problem is almost certainly hypoxia and this issue should immediately be addressed through the application of a face mask and the delivery of oxygen. Only then should the other measures be undertaken.

The presentation of hypoxia can be acute or chronic, acute manifestations include dyspnoea and tachypnoea, as well as neurologic manifestations such as restlessness, headache and confusion. In severe cases, altered mental status and coma can occur, leading quickly to death if not corrected.

The aetiology of hypoxia is vast, but broadly may be related to either low blood flow to the tissue, or low oxygen content in the blood (hypoxaemia). Causes of hypoxaemia include hypoventilation, ventilation-perfusion mismatch, right to left shunt or impaired diffusion of oxygen across the alveolar membrane. In this patient's case, there is likely an underlying pneumonia which is causing a ventilation-perfusion mismatch, leading to hypoxia.

Evaluation of acute hypoxia should include a pulse oximetry to evaluate the arterial saturation of oxygen with a formal arterial blood gas. Imaging such as chest X-ray or CT scan may be useful in determining the underlying aetiology.

Despite this, management of hypoxia must precede investigations. Management can be divided into two main categories, firstly maintaining a patent airway, which can be achieved with simple airway manoeuvres as well as more definitive airway adjuncts. Secondly, management involves increasing the fraction of inspired oxygen (FiO2) through the application of supplemental oxygen.

For this patient, as the airway is patent, the most important next step is the application of supplemental oxygen.
learningPoints: Hypoxia is a common cause of post-operative confusion.
references: Bhutta BS, Alghoula F, Berim I. Anoxia.  StatPearls. Treasure Island (FL)2021
Swarbrick CJ, Partridge JSL. Evidence-based strategies to reduce the incidence of postoperative delirium: a narrative review Anaesthesia. 2022; 77 Suppl 1:92-101.
